primary_diagnosis.name: Acute severe asthma exacerbation with reliever failure and impending respiratory fatigue

pathophysiology_summary: A viral upper-respiratory infection has triggered acute-on-chronic airway inflammation in a known asthmatic with poor preventer adherence. Bronchial smooth-muscle spasm, mucosal oedema and mucus plugging cause diffuse expiratory airflow obstruction, air trapping and hyperinflation. Repeated beta-2 agonist dosing with diminishing effect (reliever failure) signals severe, poorly reversible bronchospasm and impending respiratory-muscle fatigue; as the patient tires, minute ventilation falls, hypoxaemia worsens and a rising (rather than the early low) CO2 heralds life-threatening deterioration. The airflow limitation, not anxiety, drives the tachypnoea, tachycardia, accessory-muscle use, pulsus paradoxus and inability to complete sentences. Untreated, the pathway is towards a silent chest, exhaustion, hypercapnic respiratory failure and arrest.

differential.evidence_basis: Known asthmatic with a viral trigger, expiratory wheeze, chest tightness and reliever use.
Wheeze and acute breathlessness can be anaphylaxis — must screen for allergen exposure, urticaria and angioedema.
Acute dyspnoea and tachycardia — must be considered, though gradual onset with widespread wheeze is atypical.
Cardiac wheeze can mimic asthma; look for orthopnoea, basal crepitations, raised JVP and oedema.
The patient is highly anxious and frightened, which can present with breathlessness — but this cannot explain objective hypoxia or wheeze.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, asthma leads but the not-to-miss mimics (anaphylaxis, PE) and the anxiety red herring must all be actively screened, not assumed away.

differential.evidence_basis: Viral trigger, expiratory wheeze, reliever failure and speech limitation in a known asthmatic.
No allergen exposure, no urticaria, no lip/tongue/facial angioedema and no sudden onset.
Anxiety is prominent, but objective hypoxia (SpO2 93%) and audible wheeze argue strongly against a primary panic attack — attributing the whole picture to anxiety would be a dangerous anchor.
Still worth keeping in mind, but 6-hour progressive onset with diffuse polyphonic wheeze and a clear asthma/viral picture makes it less likely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The pivotal move is refusing the anxiety anchor: objective hypoxia plus wheeze plus reliever failure is severe asthma until proven otherwise.

differential.diagnosis: acute severe asthma exacerbation with reliever failure
differential.evidence_basis: Reliever failure, speech in short phrases, accessory-muscle use and SpO2 93% define acute severe (near-life-threatening) asthma.
No basal crepitations, no raised JVP and no peripheral oedema.
Gradual onset, widespread polyphonic wheeze and a viral/asthma picture without pleuritic pain make PE unlikely.
Objective hypoxia and airflow obstruction cannot be a panic attack; anxiety is a consequence of hypoxia and air hunger.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage the diagnosis and its severity are settled; the remaining task is correct escalation, not further differentiation.

differential.diagnosis: acute severe asthma exacerbation with reliever failure and impending respiratory fatigue
differential.evidence_basis: Time-critical: failing bronchodilator response with hypoxia and speech limitation needs nebulised/oxygen therapy, systemic steroids and objective monitoring that telehealth cannot provide — emergency transport is required.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final answer is severe asthma; the case's teeth are in triage and telehealth reprojection, not diagnostic subtlety.

red_flags.description: Reliever failure — salbutamol used ~6 times in 3 hours with diminishing effect
red_flags.clinical_significance: Poorly reversible bronchospasm and a hallmark of acute severe / near-fatal asthma; signals impending respiratory-muscle fatigue.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-triage as a routine flare-up; progression to exhaustion, hypercapnic respiratory failure and arrest.

red_flags.description: Unable to complete full sentences — speaking in short phrases
red_flags.clinical_significance: A recognised marker of severe/life-threatening asthma severity.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to appreciate life-threatening severity and to escalate to an ambulance.

red_flags.description: Accessory-muscle use, tachypnoea and tripod posture
red_flags.clinical_significance: Markedly increased work of breathing indicating severe airflow obstruction.

red_flags.description: Hypoxaemia — SpO2 93% on a home oximeter
red_flags.clinical_significance: Borderline hypoxia in acute asthma; SpO2 below 92% would indicate life-threatening asthma requiring controlled oxygen.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Silent hypoxaemia worsens toward respiratory failure while the patient is falsely reassured.

red_flags.description: Silent chest / disappearance of the audible wheeze with continuing distress
red_flags.clinical_significance: A quiet chest in a distressed asthmatic is ominous — it means airflow is too low to generate a wheeze, a peri-arrest sign.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Interpreting the loss of wheeze as improvement (the silent-chest paradox) and standing down when the patient is deteriorating.

red_flags.description: Cyanosis (blue lips), drowsiness, confusion or exhaustion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signs of life-threatening asthma and impending respiratory arrest.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing the transition to peri-arrest; fatal delay.

red_flags.clinical_significance: A clinician-measured marker of severe airflow obstruction; cannot be assessed via telehealth chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to the AI Doctor — sealed clinician sign, recorded for completeness of the true severity picture.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient is visibly anxious and frightened, inviting a 'it's just a panic attack, breathe slowly' response. Objective hypoxia, audible wheeze and reliever failure make that a dangerous misattribution — anxiety is the consequence of air hunger, not the cause.
Treating repeated reliever use as reassuring self-management rather than recognising diminishing response as a life-threatening flag of acute severe asthma.
Being reassured when the wheeze quietens or after transient improvement. A silent chest is worse, not better, and rebound deterioration is common — escalation still stands.

Attempting to manage at home. Definitive care needs nebulised bronchodilators, controlled oxygen, systemic corticosteroids and objective monitoring (PEFR/ABG) that telehealth cannot deliver — the correct action is asthma first aid plus immediate 000.

risk_scores.tool_name: Acute asthma severity assessment (National Asthma Council / Australian Asthma Handbook)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Acute severe asthma (bordering life-threatening): talks in words/short phrases, accessory-muscle use, SpO2 92–94%, poor response to reliever
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Mandates urgent face-to-face treatment and emergency transport; life-threatening features (silent chest, SpO2 <92%, exhaustion, cyanosis) would upgrade further.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultation of air entry and polyphonic expiratory wheeze
Objective peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) versus baseline
Precise blood pressure, respiratory rate and pulsus paradoxus
Arterial blood gas (e.g. respiratory alkalosis early, rising CO2 late)
True degree of airflow obstruction and response to nebulised therapy
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 for an emergency ambulance now; while waiting, coach asthma first aid — reliever via a spacer (4 puffs, one breath at a time, repeat every 4 minutes; up to 6–12 puffs), keep the patient sitting upright and calm. Paramedics provide nebulised salbutamol/ipratropium, controlled oxygen and transport.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (classic severe asthma); the real risk is UNDER-action — labelling it anxiety, being reassured by transient improvement or the silent-chest paradox, and failing to escalate.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: For a severe attack (reliever not helping, difficulty speaking, blue lips), call 000 and give reliever via spacer 4 puffs at a time, one breath each, repeating every 4 minutes until the ambulance arrives.

guidelines_referenced.reference: National Asthma Council Australia — Australian Asthma Handbook
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute severe asthma with poor reliever response requires oxygen to target saturation, repeated/continuous nebulised bronchodilators, ipratropium and early systemic corticosteroids in a monitored setting.
